key_insight: 7T whole-brain MRSI separated motor-cortex neuronal loss from spatially patterned glutamatergic dysregulation in ALS, with glutamine-related measures emerging as the most informative signal.
clinical_relevance: These imaging biomarkers may help track ALS progression and support future trial endpoint selection, but they are research tools rather than established clinical diagnostics.
method: High-resolution 7T 3D-CRT-FID-MRSI plus motor-cortex single-voxel sLASER was acquired in 5 people with ALS and 7 controls, with up to 3 sessions each.
Bayesian hierarchical mixed-effects models compared regional tNAA, Glu, Gln, and Glx metrics across motor-somatotopic subregions.
The primary motor cortex was divided into bulbar/face, hand/upper-limb, and foot/lower-limb zones to test somatotopic patterns.
evidence_signal: At baseline, ALS showed a motor-cortex tNAA/tCr deficit and increased Gln/tCr and Glx/tNAA, with Gln/tCr appearing more sensitive than Glu/tCr; annualized metabolite change correlated with ALSFRS-R decline.
watch_item: The cohort was very small and limited to rapidly progressing participants, so findings need larger, independent validation before any broader implementation.
paper_title: Glutamine and NAA dissociate in ALS across somatotopically defined motor regions using 7T MRSI
